sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Opiate toxicity (or opioid overdose) occurs when the ingestion or administration of opioids exceeds the individual's tolerance, leading to life-threatening central nervous system (CNS) and respiratory depression. Respiratory failure may lead to death. The aim of the responder is to ensure good supportive care (+/- naloxone) as this will ensure survival.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Hypoxia, cyanosis, bradypnoea, apnoea
- Hypotension, bradycardia
- Miosis (constricted pupils)
- Slurred speech
- Confusion, sedation, coma
- Hypothermia
- Pulmonary oedema (late stage or severe cases)
- Decreased bowel sounds
- Seizures (Tramadol toxicity)
- Serotonin toxicity in tramadol and oxycodone

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Investigating why or how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm and mixed overdose
- In the apnoeic or severely hypoventilating patient, prioritise oxygenation and ventilation before or alongside naloxone
- If naloxone is given intravenously, titrate it carefully to effect and avoid unnecessarily large doses that may precipitate acute withdrawal or agitation
- Drug paraphernalia, medication packets, or witness history may give important early clues to the cause
- Naloxone may wear off before the opioid does. All opioid-affected patients should be referred for further assessment and observation, consent notwithstanding

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position the patient to protect the airway where possible
- Maintain and manage the airway as required
- Provide assisted ventilation with oxygen where necessary
- Consider intramuscular naloxone if adequate ventilation alone is not restoring safe breathing
- Monitor respiratory rate, depth of breathing, mental state, oxygen saturation, and BGL
- Where available, use capnography because oxygen saturation may remain normal early in hypoventilation, especially if supplemental oxygen has been given
- Consider Secondary Survey
- Have a low threshold for contacting the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management if indicated
- Consider IV access
- Consider IV fluids only if clinically indicated
- Consider titrated IV naloxone if no previous IM dose was given and there is inadequate response to ventilation and initial care
- Titrate naloxone to reversal of respiratory depression and restoration of protective airway reflexes, not necessarily to full wakefulness
- Consider ECG, especially if co-ingestion, methadone exposure, or other toxicology features are suspected

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Although highly unlikely to be encountered on site, methadone can cause QT prolongation
- If required, ensure patient is given details for Mind Matters or EAP services
